1:
"And certain men which came down from Judaea taught the brethren, and said, Except ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, ye cannot be saved." The witnesses note the opening of the great controversy that would dominate the apostolic age. The puritan Henry and the witnesses observe that Judaizing teachers came to Antioch (the Gentile church) and taught that circumcision and the keeping of Moses' law were necessary for salvation — directly contradicting the gospel of salvation by grace through faith.

The grammatical voices note the dogmatic "cannot be saved." These teachers tied salvation to the law, making circumcision essential — not merely a custom but a condition of salvation. This struck at the very heart of the gospel, for it made works the ground of acceptance.

The Reformed Calvin (under v.2) marks the gravity: "if the keeping of the law be necessary, man's salvation is tied to works, which must be grounded in the grace of Christ alone... the worship of the law set against the free righteousness of faith." The error, seemingly tolerable, in truth overthrew the grace of Christ and the liberty of the gospel.

The thin comment matches the verse, which opens the controversy. The witnesses agree: Judaizing teachers came from Judea to Antioch, teaching that circumcision and the law of Moses were necessary for salvation — "except ye be circumcised... ye cannot be saved." This struck at the heart of the gospel, tying salvation to works and the law rather than to grace through faith (Calvin). The verse displays the first great doctrinal crisis of the church: whether the Gentiles must keep the law to be saved, or whether salvation is by grace alone. The verse marks the beginning of the Jerusalem Council narrative — the controversy that would compel the church to define the gospel of grace against the Judaizers. The error was no small matter: it would have made Christianity a sect of Judaism, bound the Gentiles to the law, and overthrown the free righteousness of faith. The verse sets the stage for the great decision: the apostles and elders would affirm that the Gentiles are saved by grace, not by the law — the charter of Christian liberty. The Judaizers' demand, "ye cannot be saved" without circumcision, is the challenge that the gospel of grace must answer; and the Council's answer would secure the freedom of the church from the yoke of the law.
2:
"When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and disputation with them, they determined that Paul and Barnabas, and certain other of them, should go up to Jerusalem unto the apostles and elders about this question." The witnesses note the earnest controversy and the decision to refer it to Jerusalem. On the "dissension," Barnes notes it was earnest, warm discussion, not improper temper: "Important principles were to be settled... it is right to contend earnestly for the faith (Jude 3)." Paul and Barnabas, who had seen God's grace among the Gentiles, firmly resisted the Judaizers.

On the reference to Jerusalem, the witnesses note its significance. Ellicott: against the Roman theory, "the reference should have been to Peter, and to Peter alone... it was a recognition of the authority of the mother-Church of Jerusalem." The witnesses identify this journey with Galatians 2:1 (by revelation, with Titus the test case).

The Reformed Calvin draws out the lawful use of councils: "the apostles took nothing to themselves imperiously, but did reverently follow that which was prescribed in the word of God... all holy synods assembled since the beginning, that grave men might decide controversies, not after their own pleasure, but according to the authority of God." He notes Paul did not depend on the apostles' verdict (Galatians 1:8) but sought their concurrence for the church's peace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul and Barnabas, after earnest controversy with the Judaizers (warm but not improper — it is right to contend for the faith, Barnes, Jude 3), were sent with others to Jerusalem to refer the question to the apostles and elders — the journey identified with Galatians 2:1 (Titus the test case, Ellicott). The reference to the whole body of apostles and elders, not to Peter alone, tells against the Roman theory (Ellicott); and Calvin draws the lawful use of councils: to decide controversies by the authority of God's word, not their own pleasure. The verse displays the church's method of settling doctrinal disputes: not by private contention alone but by common counsel under the word. The verse marks the convening of the Jerusalem Council: the great question — whether the Gentiles must keep the law — referred to the apostles and elders for decision. The verse displays both the propriety of earnest contention for the truth (Paul and Barnabas resisting the Judaizers) and the wisdom of seeking the church's common judgment. The dispute, far from a sign of the church's weakness, was the occasion of the great decision that would secure the gospel of grace and the liberty of the Gentiles.
3:
"And being brought on their way by the church, they passed through Phenice and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the Gentiles: and they caused great joy unto all the brethren." The witnesses note the journey and the joy it brought. On the route through Phenice and Samaria, Ellicott notes its significance: "The very journey was an assertion of the principles for which they were contending," passing through regions where there were established Christian societies, including Canaanites and Samaritans.

On "declaring the conversion of the Gentiles," the witnesses note the joyful report. Barnes: "These remarkable events they would naturally communicate with joy." The conversion of the Gentiles, a marvel of God's grace, was told to the brethren along the way.

On the "great joy," the witnesses note the contrast with the Pharisaic narrowness. Ellicott: "the tidings of the conversion of the Gentiles were received by the disciples at large with a gladness which presented the strongest possible contrast to the narrowness and bitterness of the Pharisee section." Barnes draws the test: "Where Christians are themselves awake... they rejoice at the news of the conversion of sinners."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul and Barnabas, sent on their way by the church, passed through Phenice and Samaria declaring the conversion of the Gentiles, causing great joy among the brethren. The route itself asserted the principle they contended for (Ellicott), passing through regions of Canaanites and Samaritans. The joy of the disciples at the Gentiles' conversion stood in strong contrast to the Pharisaic narrowness (Ellicott) — a test of true Christian feeling (Barnes). The verse displays the gladness of the church at the gospel's advance: the conversion of the Gentiles, a marvel of grace, received with joy by the brethren. The verse marks the journey to the Council as itself a witness to the gospel of grace: the apostles, telling of the Gentiles' conversion, gathered joy along the way. The contrast between the rejoicing disciples and the contending Pharisees displays the two spirits in the church: the one rejoicing at God's grace to all, the other clinging to the law and the privilege of Israel. The verse displays the proper response to the gospel's advance — joy — and prepares for the Council's affirmation of the Gentiles' freedom. The conversion of the Gentiles, far from a cause of dispute among the awake, is the occasion of great joy.
4:
"And when they were come to Jerusalem, they were received of the church, and of the apostles and elders, and they declared all things that God had done with them." The witnesses note the reception at Jerusalem and the report of God's work. On the reception, the grammatical voices note a public welcome. Robertson: "a public reception for Paul and Barnabas provided by the whole church including the apostles and elders." Ellicott notes the three bodies — the church (assembly), the elders (senate), and the apostles (council).

On "all things that God had done with them," the witnesses note the report ascribed all to God. Calvin: "God is not made a fellow-laborer, but all the whole praise of the work is ascribed to him." The apostles reported the conversion of the Gentiles as God's doing, not their own.

The witnesses note this was a preliminary meeting (Galatians 2:2). Robertson and Ellicott note Paul set before them the gospel he preached among the Gentiles. The public reception and report prepared for the formal Council (v.6).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul and Barnabas, come to Jerusalem, were received by the church, apostles, and elders, and declared all that God had done with them — a public welcome (Robertson) at which the conversion of the Gentiles was reported, all ascribed to God (Calvin). The verse displays the church's structure (church, elders, apostles, Ellicott) and the apostles' humble report: God, not they, had wrought the conversion of the Gentiles. The verse marks the arrival at Jerusalem and the preliminary meeting (Galatians 2:2): the apostles set before the church the gospel and the work among the Gentiles. The verse displays both the courteous reception of Paul and Barnabas and the proper attribution of the work to God. The report — all that God had done — is the testimony on which the Council would deliberate: the manifest grace of God among the Gentiles, the evidence that they were received without the law. The verse prepares for the formal debate (v.6) by laying before the church the facts of God's work — the foundation of the decision that the Gentiles are saved by grace. The apostles, received with honour, give glory to God for the harvest among the nations.
5:
"But there rose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed, saying, That it was needful to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses." The witnesses note the Pharisaic believers who pressed the demand for the law. The grammatical voices note these were genuine believers in Christ yet still of the Pharisee party. Robertson: "They were believers in Christ, but were still members of 'the sect of the Pharisees.' Evidently they still held to the Pharisaic narrowness."

Ellicott describes their position: "accepting Jesus as a teacher sent from God... the Head of a kingdom which was to present to mankind a restored and glorified Judaism, the Law kept in its completeness... Gentiles admitted only on their confessing their inferiority." Titus's case (a Gentile, uncircumcised) made the issue crucial (Galatians 2:1-3).

The Reformed Calvin notes the relics of their former nature: "though they had given Christ their names, yet there remained relics of their former nature. We know how proud the Pharisees were... all which they would have forgotten if they had truly put on Christ." Their old pride and addiction to external rites survived their conversion.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: certain Pharisee believers rose up, demanding that the Gentiles be circumcised and keep the law of Moses — genuine believers yet still of the Pharisee party, retaining the old narrowness (Robertson). Their position: Christianity as a restored Judaism, the Gentiles admitted only on accepting the law (Ellicott), with Titus's case the crucial test (Galatians 2). Calvin notes the relics of their former nature: the old pride and addiction to external rites survived their conversion. The verse displays the persistence of the law-bound mindset even among believers: the Pharisaic Christians, unable to shake off their old narrowness, pressed the law upon the Gentiles. The verse marks the formal raising of the issue at Jerusalem: the demand that the Gentiles keep the law — the very question the Council must settle. The verse displays how converted men may yet retain unconverted prejudices: these believers in Christ still clung to the law as the condition of salvation. The demand, sincere but mistaken, is the challenge the Council answers: the Gentiles need not keep the law, for salvation is by grace. The verse sets the formal terms of the great debate — circumcision and the law, or grace and faith.
6:
"And the apostles and elders came together for to consider of this matter." The witnesses note the formal convening of the Council. The puritan Henry and the witnesses observe the gravity and order of the assembly: the apostles and elders met deliberately to consider the great question — the first ecclesiastical council, settling the doctrine of grace against the law.

The grammatical voices note the careful deliberation. This was no hasty decree but a formal consideration, with full debate (v.7) before the decision. The apostles, though inspired, allowed the matter to be fully aired and argued.

The witnesses note the model of a true council. The apostles and elders came together to consider the matter by the word of God — the pattern of lawful synods (Calvin, under v.2): grave men deciding controversies according to God's authority, not their own pleasure. The verse opens the account of the Council's deliberations (vv.7-21).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which convenes the Council. The witnesses agree: the apostles and elders came together to consider the great question — the formal convening of the first ecclesiastical council (Henry), settling the doctrine of grace against the law. The deliberation was careful, with full debate (v.7) before decision: the apostles, though inspired, allowed the matter to be fully argued. The verse displays the model of a true council: grave men deciding controversies by the word of God, in order and deliberation. The verse marks the formal assembly of the Jerusalem Council: the apostles and elders met to consider whether the Gentiles must keep the law. The verse displays the church's method of settling doctrine: not by decree alone but by deliberate consideration, full debate, and decision according to Scripture. The Council, the pattern of all lawful synods, would weigh the question carefully (vv.7-12) before James pronounced the judgment (vv.13-21). The verse, brief as it is, marks a pivotal moment: the church gathered to define the gospel of grace and the liberty of the Gentiles. The careful, deliberate convening — apostles and elders together — displays the seriousness with which the church approached the great question of salvation by grace or by law.
7:
"And when there had been much disputing, Peter rose up, and said unto them, Men and brethren, ye know how that a good while ago God made choice among us, that the Gentiles by my mouth should hear the word of the gospel, and believe." The witnesses note Peter's appeal to the conversion of Cornelius. On "much disputing," Barnes notes it was earnest inquiry, not necessarily angry: "It might have been calm, solemn, deliberate inquiry." The Judaizers were given full opportunity to argue (Robertson).

On Peter rising, the witnesses note his fitness to speak. Robertson: "It was fitting that Peter should speak. This is the last time that Peter appears in the Acts." He appeals to his own experience at Caesarea (Acts 10) — God's choice to give the Gentiles the gospel by his mouth.

The Reformed Calvin outlines Peter's argument: "first, he proveth by the authority of God that the Gentiles must not be enforced to keep the law; secondly, he teacheth that all man's salvation is overthrown, if the conscience be once caught in this snare." Peter grounds the decision on God's manifest act (the calling of the Gentiles at Caesarea).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after much debate (earnest inquiry, the Judaizers fully heard, Barnes, Robertson), Peter rose — his last appearance in Acts (Robertson) — and appealed to the conversion of Cornelius: God chose that the Gentiles should hear the gospel by his mouth and believe. Peter grounds the decision on God's manifest act (Acts 10), the first stage of his argument (Calvin): the Gentiles must not be forced to keep the law, for God himself received them without it. The verse displays Peter's decisive testimony: the calling of the Gentiles at Caesarea, by God's own choice, settles the question. The verse marks Peter's contribution to the Council: the appeal to the evidence of God's grace among the Gentiles, received without the law. Peter, who had himself learned the lesson at Caesarea (Acts 10-11), now applies it to the Council: God made no distinction, giving the Gentiles the gospel and the Spirit apart from the law. The verse displays the foundation of the Council's decision — not human reasoning but God's manifest act. Peter's stand with Paul and Barnabas for Gentile freedom, on the ground of God's own choice, silences the Judaizers' demand and clears the way for the gospel of grace.
8:
"And God, which knoweth the hearts, bare them witness, giving them the Holy Ghost, even as he did unto us;" The witnesses note God's testimony to the Gentiles by giving them the Spirit. On "which knoweth the hearts," the grammatical voices note the characteristic Petrine epithet (Robertson, as in 1:24). God, who searches the hearts, knew the sincerity of the Gentile converts and bore witness to them.

On "giving them the Holy Ghost," the witnesses note the decisive evidence. Robertson: "And before their baptism. This was the Lord's doing." Clarke: God "evidently sent the Gospel to the Gentiles, and, by the preaching of it, conveyed the Holy Spirit to them who believed." The gift of the Spirit was God's seal upon the Gentiles' acceptance.

On "even as he did unto us," the witnesses note the equality. God gave the Gentiles the same Spirit as the Jews — making no distinction. The "Gentile Pentecost" (Acts 10:44-46) was God's own testimony that the Gentiles were received apart from the law.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God, who knows the hearts (the characteristic Petrine epithet, Robertson), bore witness to the Gentiles by giving them the Holy Spirit, even as to the Jews — the decisive evidence of their acceptance (Robertson, Clarke). The gift of the Spirit (at Caesarea, Acts 10:44-46, even before baptism) was God's seal upon the Gentiles' faith, making no distinction between Jew and Gentile. The verse displays the heart of Peter's argument: God himself, who knows the hearts, testified to the Gentiles by the gift of the Spirit — the same gift as the Jews received. The verse marks the decisive proof: if God gave the Gentiles the Spirit without the law, then the law is not necessary to their acceptance. The verse displays God's impartiality: he searched the Gentiles' hearts, found faith, and gave them the Spirit — the very seal he gave the Jews. The argument is irresistible: God made no difference (v.9), so neither may the church impose the law. The gift of the Spirit to the Gentiles, God's own witness, settles the question of their acceptance apart from circumcision. God's testimony, not human tradition, determines who is received — and he received the Gentiles by grace.
9:
"And put no difference between us and them, purifying their hearts by faith." The witnesses note the equality of Jew and Gentile and the purifying of the heart by faith. On "no difference," the grammatical voices note God treated Jew and Gentile alike. Robertson: "In the matter of faith and conversion God treated us Jews as heathen and the heathen as Jews." The wall of separation was broken (Calvin, Ephesians 2:14).

On "purifying their hearts by faith," the witnesses note the great doctrine. Barnes: "This showed that the plan on which God was now about to show favour to men was not by external rites and ceremonies, but by a scheme which required faith as the only condition of acceptance." Robertson: "Not by works nor by ceremonies. Peter here has a thoroughly Pauline and Johannine idea of salvation."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the doctrine of justification by faith: "the office of faith to translate that unto us which is proper to Christ... faith doth not make us clean, as a virtue or quality poured into our souls; but because it receiveth that cleanness which is offered in Christ." The heart is purified not by works but by faith receiving Christ's righteousness.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God put no difference between Jew and Gentile, purifying their hearts by faith — treating both alike, breaking the wall of separation (Robertson, Calvin, Ephesians 2:14). The "purifying by faith" is the great doctrine: acceptance is not by external rites but by faith alone (Barnes, Robertson) — a thoroughly Pauline note from Peter. Calvin draws out justification by faith: the heart is purified not by works but by faith receiving Christ's righteousness. The verse displays the heart of the gospel of grace: God purifies the heart by faith, making no distinction between Jew and Gentile. The verse marks the doctrinal climax of Peter's argument: if God purifies the Gentiles' hearts by faith, without the law, then the law is not the means of cleansing. The verse displays the equality of all believers in Christ and the way of acceptance — faith, not works or ceremonies. Peter's words anticipate Paul's gospel: justification by faith, the heart purified by believing, not by the law. The verse is a cornerstone of the Council's decision and of Christian doctrine: God makes no difference, and the heart is purified by faith alone — the law profiting nothing toward acceptance with God.
10:
"Now therefore why tempt ye God, to put a yoke upon the neck of the disciples, which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear?" The witnesses note Peter's pointed challenge and the impossibility of keeping the law. On "tempt ye God," the grammatical voices note the home-thrust. Robertson: "By implying that God had made a mistake this time, though right about Cornelius... They were refusing to follow the guidance of God." Ellicott: "To tempt God was to make the experiment whether His will... or man's will... was the stronger."

On "a yoke which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear," the witnesses note the impossibility of fulfilling the law. Barnes: "the ceremonial laws and customs of the Jews... They were found to be oppressive and burdensome." The Reformed Calvin presses further to the moral impossibility: "those be too foolish who restrain unto ceremonies Peter's words, whereby the weakness of men to perform the righteousness of the heart is expressed... it is manifest that the law cannot possibly be kept." He argues at length against the Pelagian/Jerome reading that the law is keepable, citing the total depravity of nature.

The witnesses note the yoke is the law as a condition of salvation. Calvin distinguishes the law as a rule of life (a yoke to be borne willingly) from the law as exacting perfect obedience under a curse (a yoke no man can bear). To impose the latter on the Gentiles is to drown them in despair.

The witnesses agree: Peter challenged the Judaizers — "why tempt ye God?" — to impose a yoke (the law as a condition of salvation) which neither the fathers nor they could bear. To tempt God was to resist his manifest will (Robertson, Ellicott). The "yoke" is the law, which the witnesses divide on: Barnes takes it as the burdensome ceremonial law; Calvin presses to the moral impossibility — the law, exacting perfect obedience under a curse, cannot be kept by fallen man, against the Pelagian/Jerome reading. To impose it on the Gentiles is to tempt God and to drown souls in despair (Calvin). The verse displays the heart of the gospel of grace: the law cannot save, for no man can keep it; to impose it is to resist God and burden the conscience. The verse marks Peter's decisive argument: the law is an unbearable yoke, which the Jews themselves could not bear — so why impose it on the Gentiles, whom God has saved by grace? The verse displays the impossibility of salvation by the law and the necessity of grace. Peter, the apostle of the circumcision, stands with Paul for Gentile freedom from the law — for the yoke neither he nor his fathers could bear is the yoke of legal salvation, from which Christ alone delivers.
11:
"But we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ we shall be saved, even as they." The witnesses note the climax of Peter's argument: salvation by grace, Jew and Gentile alike. On "through the grace," the grammatical voices note the thoroughly Pauline doctrine. Robertson: "This thoroughly Pauline note shows that whatever hopes the Judaizers had about Peter were false. His doctrine of grace is as clear as a bell."

On "even as they," the witnesses note the equality. Barnes: "In the same manner, by the mere grace of Christ. So far from being necessary to their salvation, they [the ceremonies] are really of no use in ours." The Jews are saved the same way as the Gentiles — by grace, not by the law.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the contrast between grace and the law: "to have hope in the grace of Christ, and to be under the yoke of the law... those are justified by faith who, being free and quit from the yoke of the law, seek for salvation in the grace of Jesus Christ." Salvation by grace excludes salvation by the law.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the climax of Peter's speech. The witnesses agree: Peter concludes that "through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved, even as they" — a thoroughly Pauline doctrine of grace (Robertson), placing Jew and Gentile on the same level: both saved by grace, not by the law (Barnes). Calvin draws the contrast: grace and the law are opposed; those justified by faith are free from the yoke of the law, seeking salvation in Christ's grace alone. The verse displays the heart of the gospel: salvation by the grace of Christ, the same for Jew and Gentile. The verse marks the doctrinal climax of the Council's first stage: Peter's clear confession that all — Jew and Gentile — are saved by grace, not by the law. The verse displays the unity of the way of salvation: not two ways (law for Jews, grace for Gentiles) but one — the grace of the Lord Jesus. Peter, the apostle of the circumcision, confesses the same gospel as Paul: salvation by grace through faith, the law profiting nothing. The verse is Peter's last word in Acts and his clearest: grace alone saves, Jew and Gentile alike — the foundation of the Council's decision and the charter of the gospel of grace against the law.
12:
"Then all the multitude kept silence, and gave audience to Barnabas and Paul, declaring what miracles and wonders God had wrought among the Gentiles by them." The witnesses note the silence after Peter's speech and the report of Paul and Barnabas. On the silence, the witnesses note the effect of Peter's argument. Gill: "there was a profound silence in the whole assembly... they were all satisfied with, and by their silence acquiesced in, what Peter said." Calvin: "the Spirit of God did so reign in that assembly, that they yielded forthwith to reason."

On the report, the witnesses note Paul and Barnabas's appeal to the miracles. Ellicott: "with a special view to the character of their hearers... they laid stress on the signs and wonders which had attested God's acceptance of their work." The miracles among the Gentiles were God's seal of approval.

The grammatical voices note the order of the speakers. Robertson: "Note placing Barnabas before Paul, possibly because in Jerusalem Barnabas was still better known." The two apostles confirmed Peter's doctrine with the facts of God's work among the Gentiles.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after Peter's speech the multitude fell silent — acquiescing in his argument, the Spirit reigning in the assembly (Gill, Calvin) — and gave audience to Barnabas and Paul, who declared the miracles God had wrought among the Gentiles. The report, with its stress on the signs and wonders (God's seal of approval, Ellicott), confirmed Peter's doctrine with the facts. The verse displays the persuasive power of God's manifest work: the silence after Peter's argument, and the further evidence of the miracles, settled the question. The verse marks the second stage of the Council: Paul and Barnabas confirming, by the facts of God's work, what Peter had argued — that the Gentiles were received by grace, attested by miracles. The verse displays the harmony of doctrine and fact: Peter's argument from God's act, and Paul and Barnabas's report of God's signs, together prove the gospel of grace. The silence of the assembly marks the turning of the tide: the Judaizers' objection overcome, the church persuaded by God's own testimony. The verse prepares for James's judgment (vv.13-21): the facts established, the doctrine confirmed, the decision could now be reached. God's work among the Gentiles, attested by miracles, is the ground of the Council's affirmation of grace.
13:
"And after they had held their peace, James answered, saying, Men and brethren, hearken unto me:" The witnesses note James's authoritative summing-up — and the anti-papal point. Chrysostom marks James's position: "This (James) was bishop, as they say, and therefore he speaks last... His also is a more complete oration, as indeed it puts the completion to the matter under discussion." James, as president, spoke last and gave the decisive judgment.

The witnesses draw the anti-papal conclusion. Barnes: "This whole transaction shows that Peter had no such authority in the church as the Papists pretend, for otherwise his opinion would have been followed without debate. James had an authority not less than that of Peter." Clarke: "Had Peter been prince and head of the apostles... he would have appeared here in the character of judge." Calvin: "the impudency of the Pope is hereby refuted, because the decree of the Council is set down rather at the appointment of James than of Peter."

The witnesses note James's identity (James the Just, the Lord's brother, son of Alpheus, called an apostle in Galatians 1:19) and his presiding role. Robertson: "It was expected that James, as President of the Conference, would speak last."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: James, as president of the Council, spoke last and summed up the matter — his oration completing the discussion (Chrysostom). The witnesses draw the anti-papal conclusion: that James, not Peter, gave the decisive judgment refutes the Roman claim of Peter's primacy (Barnes, Clarke, Calvin) — had Peter been the head, his word would have settled it without debate. James (the Lord's brother, called an apostle, Galatians 1:19) presided and pronounced the verdict. The verse displays the authority of James at Jerusalem and the collegial nature of the Council: not Peter's primacy but the common judgment, summed up by James. The verse marks the decisive stage of the Council: James, having heard Peter and Paul and Barnabas, gives the authoritative judgment (vv.14-21). The verse displays the church's order: the president summing up after full debate, the decision reached by common counsel. The prominence of James — not Peter — at the first council is, the witnesses note, a standing refutation of the papal claim. James's "hearken unto me" introduces the verdict that would settle the question of the Gentiles and the law — grounded, as the witnesses show (vv.14-18), in Scripture and the manifest act of God.
14:
"Simeon hath declared how God at the first did visit the Gentiles, to take out of them a people for his name." The witnesses note James's confirmation of Peter's testimony. On "Simeon," the grammatical voices note the Aramaic form of Simon (Peter). Robertson: "This little touch would show his affinities with the Jewish Christians." James refers to Peter's account of Cornelius (v.7).

On "did visit the Gentiles," the witnesses note the gracious visitation. James affirms that God, at the first (in the case of Cornelius), visited the Gentiles in mercy, taking from them a people for his name — the first stage of the calling of the Gentiles.

On "a people for his name," the witnesses note the great truth. Ellicott: "The Jews claimed for themselves the exclusive right to the latter term... St. James proclaims that out of those heathen nations a people had been taken who were as truly God's people as Israel had ever been." Robertson: "a chosen people out of the Gentiles." The Gentiles are now God's people, called by his name.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: James confirms Peter's testimony — "Simeon hath declared how God did visit the Gentiles, to take out of them a people for his name." James affirms that God, at the first (Cornelius, v.7), visited the Gentiles in mercy, taking from them a people for his name (Robertson). The "people for his name" is the great truth: the Gentiles, formerly the "nations," are now as truly God's people as Israel (Ellicott). The verse displays James's agreement with Peter and the doctrine of the Gentile calling: God has taken a people from the Gentiles, calling them by his name. The verse marks the foundation of James's argument: the manifest act of God in visiting the Gentiles (Cornelius) is the fact, which the prophets confirm (vv.15-18). The verse displays the new thing God had done: the Gentiles, once outside the covenant, now a people for his name — the Israel of God drawn from all nations. James, the representative of the Hebraic Christians, confirms the calling of the Gentiles: God himself has taken a people from them, making no distinction. The verse prepares for the scriptural proof from Amos (vv.16-17): the calling of the Gentiles, attested by God's act and the prophets, is the ground of the Council's decision.
15:
"And to this agree the words of the prophets; as it is written," The witnesses note James's appeal to the prophets to confirm the calling of the Gentiles. On "the words of the prophets," the grammatical voices note James cites Amos 9:11-12 as representative. Barnes: "It was a very material point with them, as Jews, to inquire whether this was in accordance with the predictions of the Scriptures."

On the appeal to Scripture, Barnes draws the lesson: "The most powerful revivals of religion... will be in accordance with the Bible, and should be tested by it... Unless a supposed work of grace accords with the Bible... it must be false." The fact of the Gentile calling must be confirmed by the prophets.

The Reformed Calvin notes the apostles' submission to Scripture: "the apostles took nothing to themselves imperiously, but did reverently follow that which was prescribed in the word of God... they did simply aim at this, that they might prove that their doctrine was taken out of the word of God." James cites the prophets (plural) though quoting one, for all agree.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: James appeals to "the words of the prophets" (citing Amos 9:11-12 as representative) to confirm the calling of the Gentiles — testing the fact by Scripture (Barnes). The appeal displays the principle that even the most striking work of God must accord with the Bible (Barnes); and the apostles' submission to Scripture, proving their doctrine from the word (Calvin). The verse displays the method of the Council: not deciding by experience alone but confirming the fact by the prophets. The verse marks the scriptural foundation of James's judgment: the calling of the Gentiles, manifest in God's act (v.14), agrees with the prophets. The verse displays the church's submission to Scripture as the rule of doctrine: the fact of the Gentile calling, however manifest, is confirmed by the word of God. James, citing the prophets, grounds the Council's decision not in human reasoning but in the testimony of Scripture — the pattern of all sound doctrine. The verse introduces the Amos quotation (vv.16-17), the prophetic proof that the Gentiles were always part of God's purpose. The agreement of the prophets with the manifest act of God settles the question: the calling of the Gentiles is no novelty but the fulfillment of prophecy.
16:
"After this I will return, and will build again the tabernacle of David, which is fallen down; and I will build again the ruins thereof, and I will set it up:" The witnesses note the citation of Amos 9:11 and its application to Christ's kingdom. On the "tabernacle of David," the grammatical voices note it figures the throne or kingdom of David. Robertson: "a poetical figure of the throne of David... now the fallen tent." The fallen and rebuilt tabernacle of David is the kingdom of the Messiah, the Son of David.

On the application, Ellicott notes: "It assumes that the tabernacle of David, which to human eyes had been lying as in ruins, was being rebuilt by Christ, the Son of David." The witnesses (Lightfoot, Clarke) cite the Jewish tradition that this passage was understood of the Messiah (Bar Naphli).

The Reformed Calvin draws out the meaning: the prophecy of the restored tabernacle "doth properly appertain unto Christ... so soon as the kingdom of Christ is set up, that must needs follow which the prophet saith also, that the Gentiles shall call upon the name of God." The rebuilding of David's fallen house is the establishment of Christ's kingdom, which includes the Gentiles.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: James cites Amos 9:11 — God will rebuild the fallen tabernacle of David — applying it to Christ's kingdom. The "tabernacle of David" figures the throne or kingdom of David (Robertson), now rebuilt by Christ the Son of David (Ellicott); the Jewish tradition understood it of the Messiah (Lightfoot, Clarke). Calvin draws the meaning: the rebuilding of David's fallen house is the establishment of Christ's kingdom, which includes the Gentiles (v.17). The verse displays the prophetic foundation of the Gentile calling: the Messiah's kingdom, the rebuilt house of David, embraces the Gentiles. The verse marks the scriptural proof that the Gentiles were always part of God's purpose: the prophet foretold that in the Messiah's kingdom the Gentiles would seek the Lord. The verse displays the typology of David's house: fallen in the exile and the loss of the kingdom, rebuilt in Christ, whose kingdom includes all nations. James grounds the Council's decision in this prophecy: the calling of the Gentiles is the fulfillment of Amos's word, the rebuilding of David's tabernacle in the kingdom of Christ. The Gentiles, drawn into the Messiah's kingdom, are no afterthought but the foretold fruit of the restored house of David.
17:
"That the residue of men might seek after the Lord, and all the Gentiles, upon whom my name is called, saith the Lord, who doeth all these things." The witnesses note the heart of the Amos prophecy — the Gentiles seeking the Lord — and the LXX reading. On "the residue of men... all the Gentiles," the grammatical voices note this is the key clause. Barnes: "This was a clear prediction that other nations were to be favoured with the light of the true religion... without any mention of their conforming to the rites of the Jewish people."

On the LXX reading, the witnesses note the difference from the Hebrew. Ellicott: the argument "mainly turns on a clause in which the LXX version, which St. James quotes, differs from the Hebrew" (the Hebrew "possess the remnant of Edom" vs the LXX "the residue of men seek the Lord") — "shows, beyond the shadow of a doubt, that the discussion must have been conducted in Greek, and not in Hebrew." Clarke and Barnes discuss the textual variation (Edom/Adam, possess/seek).

On "upon whom my name is called," the witnesses note the Gentiles are now God's people. Robertson: "a Jewish way of speaking of those who worship God." The Gentiles, called by God's name, are included in the Messiah's kingdom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the heart of the Amos prophecy is that "the residue of men and all the Gentiles" should seek the Lord — a clear prediction of the Gentiles' inclusion without conforming to Jewish rites (Barnes). The argument turns on the LXX reading (differing from the Hebrew "possess the remnant of Edom"), proving the discussion was conducted in Greek (Ellicott). The "Gentiles upon whom my name is called" are now God's people (Robertson). The verse displays the prophetic proof of the Gentile calling: Amos foretold that in the Messiah's kingdom the Gentiles would seek the Lord and be called by his name. The verse marks the scriptural climax of James's argument: the Gentiles' inclusion, manifest in God's act (v.14), is the fulfillment of prophecy. The verse displays the universality of the Messiah's kingdom: the Gentiles, drawn to seek the Lord, are part of God's eternal purpose. The textual point (the LXX reading) confirms that the Council debated in Greek and grounded its decision in the Greek Scriptures familiar to the Hellenists. James's proof is complete: the calling of the Gentiles, foretold by Amos, fulfilled in Christ's kingdom, settles the question — the Gentiles are received as God's people, apart from the law.
18:
"Known unto God are all his works from the beginning of the world." The witnesses note James's affirmation that the Gentile calling was God's eternal purpose, no novelty. On "known unto God all his works," the grammatical voices note the answer to the charge of innovation. Ellicott: "an implicit answer to the charge of innovation. If the work were of God, it could not be so called, for His mercies are everlasting, and the work which He carries on now must be thought of as contemplated and purposed from eternity."

Barnes draws the meaning: "God sees everything future; he knows what he will accomplish; he has a plan... As he foretold these, it was a part of his plan; and as it was a part of his plan long since foretold, it should not be opposed and resisted by us." The calling of the Gentiles, foretold and eternally purposed, is not to be resisted.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley draws out the foreknowledge of contingencies: "this conversion of the Gentiles being known to him from eternity, we ought not to think a new or strange thing... This text is an unanswerable proof that God foreknows future contingencies." God's eternal knowledge of his works includes the free acts of men.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: James affirms that "known unto God are all his works from the beginning" — the calling of the Gentiles was God's eternal purpose, no novelty (Ellicott). The work, foretold and eternally planned, is not to be resisted (Barnes); and Wesley draws the foreknowledge of contingencies — God's eternal knowledge includes the free acts of men. The verse displays the answer to the charge of innovation: the Gentile calling is no new thing but God's purpose from eternity, manifest now in its time. The verse marks the close of James's scriptural argument: the calling of the Gentiles, foretold by Amos and eternally purposed by God, is to be received, not resisted. The verse displays the eternal counsel of God: all his works, including the calling of the Gentiles, were known and planned from the beginning. The sudden change — the Gentiles received apart from the law — is no surprise to God but the unfolding of his eternal purpose. James grounds the Council's decision in the eternity of God's plan: the Gentile calling is not a departure from God's ways but their fulfillment. The verse teaches humility before God's purposes: we must not measure his works by our understanding but receive what he has foretold and now accomplishes.
19:
"Wherefore my sentence is, that we trouble not them, which from among the Gentiles are turned to God:" The witnesses note James's judgment — not to burden the Gentiles. On "my sentence," the grammatical voices note the authoritative but non-dictatorial tone. Barnes: "It is the usual language in which a judge delivers his opinion; but it does not imply here that James assumed authority to settle the case, but merely that he gave his opinion or counsel." Robertson: "James sums up the case as President of the Conference in a masterly fashion."

On "trouble not them," the witnesses note the relief from the burden of the law. Ellicott: the verb "expresses the idea of worrying or harassing." James's judgment is that the Gentile converts should not be troubled with the demand for circumcision and the law.

The Reformed Calvin notes the apparent contradiction (James prescribes four things, yet says not to trouble them): "he requireth nothing at their hands but that which they were bound to do by brotherly concord; secondly, these precepts could no whit trouble or disquiet their consciences, after that they knew that they were free before God." The four abstentions (v.20) do not burden the conscience but serve concord.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: James gives his judgment — "trouble not them which from among the Gentiles are turned to God" — relieving the Gentile converts from the burden of the law (Barnes, Ellicott). The "sentence" is authoritative but not dictatorial: James sums up as president, giving his counsel (Robertson). Calvin resolves the apparent contradiction (the four abstentions, v.20): they do not burden the conscience but serve brotherly concord, the Gentiles being free before God. The verse displays the heart of the Council's decision: the Gentiles are not to be troubled with circumcision and the law. The verse marks James's verdict: the Gentile converts, turned to God by grace, are not to be burdened with the law. The verse displays the liberty of the gospel: the Gentiles are free, not bound to the yoke of Moses. James's judgment — "trouble not them" — is the charter of Gentile freedom, securing the gospel of grace against the Judaizers. The four abstentions that follow (v.20) are not a re-imposition of the law but a concession to peace, not binding the conscience but preserving concord between Jew and Gentile in the church. The verse settles the great question: the Gentiles are received by grace, not troubled with the law.
20:
"But that we write unto them, that they abstain from pollutions of idols, and from fornication, and from things strangled, and from blood." The witnesses note the four abstentions and their rationale. On the four things — pollutions of idols (things sacrificed to idols), fornication, things strangled, and blood — the witnesses note they were a compromise. Ellicott: "It was of the nature of a compromise... The rules on which stress was now laid found a place among the seven precepts traditionally ascribed to Noah" (the Noachic precepts, binding on all mankind), and would avoid offense to the Jews.

On the inclusion of fornication (a moral matter among ceremonial ones), the witnesses explain. Barnes: it was "the vice to which all were particularly exposed... not deemed by the Gentiles disgraceful... connected with religion" (temple prostitution). Calvin notes it may refer especially to concubinage, common among the Gentiles and not held disgraceful.

On "blood" and "things strangled," the witnesses note the Mosaic and Noachic prohibition (the life in the blood, Genesis 9:4; Leviticus 17). Some (Adam Clarke) note the question whether "blood" means literal blood or murder. The abstentions, the witnesses note (Calvin), were temporary, for concord, not perpetual law.

The witnesses agree: James proposes four abstentions — pollutions of idols (things sacrificed to idols), fornication, things strangled, and blood — a compromise drawn from the Noachic precepts (binding on all mankind) to avoid offense to the Jews (Ellicott). The inclusion of fornication (a moral matter) among ceremonial ones is explained: it was the universal pagan vice, not held disgraceful, often connected with idolatrous worship (Barnes, Calvin on concubinage). The prohibition of blood and things strangled rests on the Noachic/Mosaic law (the life in the blood, Genesis 9:4). The witnesses note (Calvin) the abstentions were temporary, for concord, not perpetual law. The verse displays the wisdom of the compromise: the Gentiles, free from the law, are asked to abstain from these things to preserve peace with the Jews. The verse marks the practical terms of the Council's decision: not the law, but four abstentions for concord. The verse displays the balance of liberty and charity: the Gentiles are free, yet asked to forbear these things lest they offend the Jewish believers. The four abstentions — three ceremonial, one moral — are the conditions of fellowship, not of salvation. The verse settles the practical question: the Gentiles are received by grace, asked only to abstain from what would needlessly offend, the law's yoke removed but charity preserved.
21:
"For Moses of old time hath in every city them that preach him, being read in the synagogues every sabbath day." The witnesses note the reason for the abstentions — and divide on its sense. The common reading (Barnes, Ellicott) is that the Jews, who hear Moses read every sabbath, need no instruction; the abstentions are for the Gentiles, that they not needlessly offend the Jews who revere the law. Barnes: "As these commands were constantly read, and as the Jewish converts would not soon learn that their ceremonial law had ceased to be binding, it was deemed expedient that no needless offence should be given."

The Reformed Calvin offers an alternative reading: the ceremonies cannot be abolished all at once, for the Jews are long accustomed to them: "it cannot be that ceremonies can be abolished so quickly... because the Jews had now a long time been acquainted with the doctrine of the law... it stood them upon to redeem concord for a short time, until such time as the liberty gotten by Christ might, by little and little, appear more plainly." The old ceremonies should be buried with honour.

The witnesses note the purpose: to preserve peace between Jew and Gentile in the church. Whether the Jews need no instruction (the common view) or the ceremonies must be phased out gradually (Calvin), the abstentions serve the concord of the church during the transition.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: James gives the reason for the abstentions — "Moses hath in every city them that preach him." The common reading (Barnes, Ellicott): the Jews, hearing Moses read every sabbath, revere the law and would be offended if the Gentiles disregarded these things; the abstentions preserve peace. Calvin offers an alternative: the ceremonies cannot be abolished all at once, the Jews being long accustomed to them, so concord must be kept for a season until the liberty of Christ appears more plainly. The verse displays the rationale of the compromise: the abstentions serve the concord of the church during the transition from law to grace. The verse marks the close of James's judgment: the abstentions are for peace, not perpetual law, given because the Jews everywhere revere Moses. The verse displays the pastoral wisdom of the Council: the Gentiles, free from the law, are asked to forbear lest they offend the Jews who hear Moses every sabbath. Whether the Jews need no instruction or the ceremonies must be phased out gradually, the purpose is the same — the unity of the church during the transition. The verse settles the practical ground of the abstentions: charity toward the Jewish believers, that the gospel of grace might advance without needless offense, until the liberty of Christ should be fully understood.
22:
"Then pleased it the apostles and elders, with the whole church, to send chosen men of their own company to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas; namely, Judas surnamed Barsabas, and Silas, chief men among the brethren:" The witnesses note the unanimous decision and the choice of delegates. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the involvement of the whole church: not the apostles and elders only, but the whole church concurred in the decision and the sending of delegates.

On the delegates, the grammatical voices note Judas Barsabas and Silas were "chief men among the brethren." Robertson and Chrysottom (under v.13) note they were sent to authenticate the decree, that there be no suspicion of Paul and Barnabas alone — men of their own company, to confirm the letter.

The witnesses note the wisdom of sending these men. Chrysostom: "to authenticate the decree, they send men of their own, that there may be no room for regarding Paul and his company with suspicion." Silas would become Paul's companion on the next journey (v.40).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles, elders, and the whole church unanimously decided to send chosen delegates — Judas Barsabas and Silas, chief men among the brethren — to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas. The involvement of the whole church (Henry) marks the unanimity of the decision; and the delegates were sent to authenticate the decree, that there be no suspicion of Paul and Barnabas alone (Chrysostom). The verse displays the order and unanimity of the Council: the decision reached by common consent, the delegates chosen to confirm it. The verse marks the implementation of the Council's decision: the sending of trusted men to carry and verify the decree. The verse displays the wisdom of the church in confirming the decision by independent witnesses: Judas and Silas, chief men of Jerusalem, would attest the decree to the church at Antioch. The verse also introduces Silas, who would become Paul's companion (v.40) after the breach with Barnabas (v.39). The unanimous decision — apostles, elders, and whole church — and the sending of delegates display the church's careful, orderly resolution of the great question: the gospel of grace affirmed, the Gentiles freed, the decision confirmed by trusted witnesses.
23:
"And they wrote letters by them after this manner; The apostles and elders and brethren send greeting unto the brethren which are of the Gentiles in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia:" The witnesses note the apostolic letter and its gracious address. On the form, the grammatical voices note the standard epistolary greeting. The letter is from "the apostles and elders and brethren" — the whole church — to the Gentile brethren of Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia.

The witnesses note the warmth of the address. Chrysostom (under v.13): "mark with what forbearance of all harsh vituperation of those brethren they indite their epistle." The letter addresses the Gentile converts as "brethren," recognizing them as full members of the church.

The witnesses note the scope: Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia — the regions where the controversy had arisen and where Paul and Barnabas had labored. The letter, addressed to the Gentile brethren, secures their freedom from the law in writing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles, elders, and brethren wrote a letter to the Gentile brethren of Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia — the standard epistolary greeting, from the whole church to the Gentile converts. The warmth of the address (calling them "brethren," Chrysostom) recognizes the Gentiles as full members of the church. The verse displays the formal, gracious communication of the Council's decision: a letter securing the Gentiles' freedom from the law. The verse marks the writing of the apostolic decree: the decision put in writing and addressed to the Gentile brethren of the regions where the controversy arose. The verse displays the church's care to communicate the decision clearly and warmly: the Gentile converts, addressed as brethren, are assured of their acceptance and freedom. The letter, from the apostles, elders, and whole church, is the authoritative record of the Council's decision — the charter of Gentile liberty. The verse displays the unity of the church (apostles, elders, brethren together) and its love for the Gentile converts (addressed as brethren). The formal letter secures in writing what the Council decided: the Gentiles are received by grace, free from the law, full brethren in the church of Christ.
24:
"Forasmuch as we have heard, that certain which went out from us have troubled you with words, subverting your souls, saying, Ye must be circumcised, and keep the law: to whom we gave no such commandment:" The witnesses note the disavowal of the Judaizers. The grammatical voices note the strong language. The letter describes the Judaizers as having "troubled" and "subverted" the souls of the Gentiles with their demand for circumcision and the law.

On "to whom we gave no such commandment," the witnesses note the repudiation. Chrysostom (under v.13): "Sufficient was this charge against the temerity of those men... that they said nothing beyond this." The Judaizers had acted without authority; the apostles had given them no such commission.

The witnesses note the gravity of "subverting your souls." Barnes and others note the Judaizers' teaching, by tying salvation to the law, would have subverted the gospel of grace — overthrowing the souls of the Gentile converts. The apostles disavow them entirely.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the letter disavows the Judaizers — "certain which went out from us have troubled you... to whom we gave no such commandment." The Judaizers had troubled and subverted the souls of the Gentiles with the demand for circumcision and the law, acting without apostolic authority (Chrysostom). The strong language ("subverting your souls") marks the gravity: the Judaizers' teaching, tying salvation to the law, would have overthrown the gospel of grace. The verse displays the apostles' repudiation of the Judaizers: they had no commission for their teaching. The verse marks the heart of the apostolic letter: the disavowal of the false teachers who troubled the Gentile churches. The verse displays the church's authority to repudiate error: the Judaizers, though they came from Jerusalem, had no commission; their teaching is disowned. The verse displays the seriousness of the controversy: the Judaizers' demand was not a minor matter but a subverting of souls, an overthrowing of the gospel. The apostles, disowning them, clear the Gentile churches of the false teaching and affirm the gospel of grace. The verse secures the Gentiles against the Judaizers: the demand for the law is disavowed, the souls subverted by it are reclaimed for grace.
25:
"It seemed good unto us, being assembled with one accord, to send chosen men unto you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul," The witnesses note the unanimity of the Council and the commendation of Paul and Barnabas. On "with one accord," the grammatical voices note the unanimity. The Council, assembled with one mind, decided to send the delegates — the decision reached by common consent.

On "our beloved Barnabas and Paul," the witnesses note the warm commendation. Chrysostom (under v.13): "that it may not look like disparagement of Paul and Barnabas, that those men are sent, observe the encomium passed upon them." The letter commends Paul and Barnabas as "beloved," honoring them against the Judaizers' disparagement.

The witnesses note the order (Barnabas before Paul) as in v.12, perhaps because Barnabas was better known in Jerusalem. The Council's unanimous decision and warm commendation of the apostles secure the gospel of grace and vindicate its chief advocates.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Council, "assembled with one accord," decided to send the delegates with "our beloved Barnabas and Paul" — the unanimity of the decision (common consent) and the warm commendation of the apostles (honoring them against the Judaizers' disparagement, Chrysostom). The order (Barnabas before Paul) perhaps reflects Barnabas's greater fame in Jerusalem. The verse displays the unanimity of the Council and its honor for Paul and Barnabas: the decision reached with one accord, the chief advocates of grace commended as beloved. The verse marks the Council's vindication of Paul and Barnabas: against the Judaizers who troubled their churches, the apostles and elders call them "beloved." The verse displays both the unity of the Council (with one accord) and its esteem for the missionaries to the Gentiles. The warm commendation secures the standing of Paul and Barnabas: they are not the troublers but the beloved servants of the church, whose gospel the Council affirms. The verse displays the harmony restored: the Council, united, honors its missionaries and sends delegates to confirm the decision — the gospel of grace vindicated, its advocates commended, the Gentile churches assured. The verse displays the spirit of the Council toward the men whom the Judaizers had maligned: not suspicion but love, not censure but the title "beloved." The unanimity ("with one accord") and the affection together display a church healed of its strife, united in the gospel of grace and in honour for those who proclaimed it. The decision, reached without dissent and sealed with love, is the model of a controversy rightly resolved.
26:
"Men that have hazarded their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the high tribute to Paul and Barnabas. The grammatical voices note the strong commendation. The apostles describe Paul and Barnabas as men who had "hazarded their lives" — risked death — for the name of Christ.

The witnesses note the reference to their sufferings. Paul had been stoned at Lystra (14:19), and both had faced perils throughout the first journey. The Council honors them for their willingness to risk death for the gospel.

The tribute the witnesses read as a vindication and an honor. Against the Judaizers who troubled the churches, the apostles commend Paul and Barnabas as men who had risked their lives for Christ — the highest possible recommendation. Their devotion is set against the Judaizers' meddling.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles honor Paul and Barnabas as "men that have hazarded their lives for the name of Christ" — a high tribute, referring to their sufferings (Paul stoned at Lystra, 14:19) and perils for the gospel. The Council commends them for their willingness to risk death for Christ — the highest recommendation, set against the Judaizers' meddling. The verse displays the Council's esteem for Paul and Barnabas: not troublers but heroes of the faith, who had risked their lives for the name of Christ. The verse marks the climax of the commendation: the apostles vindicate Paul and Barnabas as men of proven devotion, who had hazarded their lives for the gospel. The verse displays the honor due to those who suffer for Christ: the Council commends the missionaries for their willingness to die for the name. The tribute secures their standing against the Judaizers and honors their sacrificial labor. The verse displays the true mark of the servants of Christ — not the imposing of the law but the hazarding of life for the gospel. Paul and Barnabas, commended for their devotion, are the model of faithful service; and the Council, by honoring them, affirms the gospel of grace for which they risked their lives.
27:
"We have sent therefore Judas and Silas, who shall also tell you the same things by mouth." The brief verse notes the sending of the delegates to confirm the letter by word. The grammatical voices note the purpose: Judas and Silas would deliver the same message verbally, confirming the written decree. Robertson and others note this authenticates the letter, that there be no doubt of its contents.

The witnesses note the wisdom of confirming the written word by living witnesses. The letter would be confirmed by the spoken testimony of Judas and Silas — trusted men of Jerusalem — so that the Gentile churches might be fully assured.

The verse marks the means of delivering the decision: the written letter and the verbal confirmation of the delegates together, that the Gentile churches might receive the decree with full confidence.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of the letter. The witnesses agree: Judas and Silas were sent to confirm the letter by word — delivering the same message verbally (Robertson), authenticating the written decree. The wisdom of confirming the written word by living witnesses ensures the Gentile churches' full assurance. The verse displays the care of the Council to deliver its decision clearly: the letter confirmed by the spoken testimony of trusted delegates. The verse marks the means of communicating the decree: written and verbal together, that there be no doubt. The verse displays the thoroughness of the Council: not content with a letter, it sends men to confirm it by mouth. The Gentile churches, receiving both the written decree and the verbal confirmation of Judas and Silas, could be fully assured of their freedom from the law. The verse, brief as it is, displays the church's diligence in communicating the gospel of grace: the decision confirmed by every means, that the Gentiles might rest secure in their liberty. The sending of Judas and Silas to confirm the letter by word is the final touch of the Council's care — the decree delivered, confirmed, and the Gentile churches assured of their acceptance by grace. The verse displays the pastoral thoroughness of the apostles: not trusting to a document alone, which might be questioned or misread, but sending living witnesses to vouch for it. The written and the spoken word together leave no room for doubt, that the Gentiles might receive the decree of liberty with full and settled confidence in their acceptance by grace.
28:
"For it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things;" The witnesses note the great claim of inspiration and the meaning of the decree. On "the Holy Ghost, and to us," the grammatical voices note the strong claim. Barnes: "This is a strong and undoubted claim to inspiration." Robertson: "Definite claim that the church in this action had the guidance of the Holy Spirit." The Council ascribes its decision to the Spirit's guidance.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the relation of the Spirit and the apostles: "the Holy Ghost was the captain, guide, and governor, and that they did set down that which they write as he did indite it to them... the apostles deny that they invented that decree of their own brain, but that they were only ministers of the Spirit." Yet he strongly rebuts the Papist use of this for conciliar authority: the necessity was temporary, "accidental or external... in avoiding of the offense," for Paul later abolished the decree (Romans 14:14; 1 Corinthians 10:25). The Council added nothing to God's word but applied the rule of charity to its time.

On "no greater burden than these necessary things," the witnesses note the lightness of the burden. Robertson: "In comparison with the freedom won this 'burden' is light." Ellicott and Wesley note the precepts were "necessary" for the time, not perpetually binding.

The witnesses agree: the decree claims the guidance of the Spirit — "it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us" — a strong claim to inspiration (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin draws out the relation: the Spirit the guide, the apostles his ministers, adding nothing to God's word but applying charity to their time; and he strongly rebuts the Papist use of this for conciliar authority, noting the necessity was temporary (for avoiding offense), the decree later abolished by Paul (Romans 14:14; 1 Corinthians 10). The "necessary things" were necessary for the time and for concord, not perpetual law (Ellicott, Wesley) — a light burden compared to the freedom won (Robertson). The verse displays the heart of the decree: the Spirit-guided decision to impose no burden but the four abstentions for concord. The verse marks the formal claim of inspiration: the Council, guided by the Spirit, lays no burden on the Gentiles but the necessary things. The verse displays both the authority of the decree (the Spirit's guidance) and its limited scope (a temporary expedient for concord, not perpetual law). Calvin's rebuttal guards against the abuse of this verse for human conciliar authority: the Council bound nothing beyond God's word but applied the rule of charity. The Gentiles are freed, the law's yoke removed, and only the necessary things for peace retained.
29:
"That ye abstain from meats offered to idols, and from blood, and from things strangled, and from fornication: from which if ye keep yourselves, ye shall do well. Fare ye well." The witnesses note the restatement of the four abstentions and the gracious close. On the four things, the witnesses note "meats offered to idols" specifies the "pollutions of idols" (v.20). The decree restates the abstentions: things sacrificed to idols, blood, things strangled, and fornication.

On the temporary vs permanent nature, Ellicott discusses at length: the decree was a concordat, framed with inspired sagacity, but Paul never refers to it even when discussing things sacrificed to idols (1 Corinthians 8-10), treating the matter on different grounds. The prohibition of blood was kept long in the Greek Church but abandoned in the West (Augustine). Hooker cites this as "a crucial instance proving that commands might be divine and yet given only for a season."

On "fare ye well," the witnesses note the Greek epistolary close (Clarke, Ellicott). Wesley draws out the gentle, non-anathematizing tone: "how soon did succeeding councils of inferior authority change it into the style of anathemas!" The decree closes with a blessing, not a curse.

The witnesses agree: the decree restates the four abstentions — meats offered to idols, blood, things strangled, fornication — with the gracious close "if ye keep yourselves, ye shall do well. Fare ye well." The witnesses discuss at length the temporary vs permanent nature (Ellicott, Wesley): the decree was a concordat for the time, not perpetual law — Paul never appeals to it even when discussing things sacrificed to idols (1 Corinthians 8-10); the blood prohibition was kept long in the Greek Church but abandoned in the West; Hooker cites it as a crucial instance that divine commands may be given only for a season. The gentle close ("fare ye well," a blessing not a curse) contrasts with the later councils' anathemas (Wesley). The verse displays the practical terms and gracious spirit of the decree: the four abstentions for concord, the close a blessing. The verse marks the heart of the apostolic decree: the Gentiles asked to abstain from these things, with a kindly farewell. The verse displays both the wisdom of the compromise (a concordat for peace) and the limited, temporary nature of the precepts (later superseded by Paul's fuller teaching). The decree, framed with inspired sagacity, secured the unity of the church during the transition from law to grace. The gentle close — "ye shall do well, fare ye well" — displays the apostolic spirit of charity, blessing rather than cursing, that marks the gospel of grace.
30:
"So when they were dismissed, they came to Antioch: and when they had gathered the multitude together, they delivered the epistle:" The witnesses note the delivery of the decree to Antioch. The grammatical voices note the gathering of the church. The delegates, dismissed from Jerusalem, came to Antioch and gathered the multitude (the whole church) to deliver the letter.

The witnesses note the return of the decision to its origin. The controversy had begun at Antioch (v.1); now the decree is delivered there, to the church that had sent the delegates to Jerusalem. The whole church is gathered to hear the apostolic decision.

The Reformed Calvin notes the orderly delivery: the decree, framed at Jerusalem, is brought back to Antioch and read to the assembled church, that all might know the decision and be assured of their freedom.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the delegates came to Antioch and gathered the whole church to deliver the apostolic letter — returning the decision to its origin (the controversy had begun at Antioch, v.1). The whole church was assembled to hear the decree. The verse displays the orderly delivery of the Council's decision: the letter brought back and read to the assembled church. The verse marks the completion of the Council's work at Antioch: the decree delivered to the church that had referred the question to Jerusalem. The verse displays the care of the church to communicate the decision to all: the whole multitude gathered to hear the letter. The decree, framed at Jerusalem and confirmed by the delegates, is now delivered to the Antioch church — the gospel of grace affirmed, the Gentiles' freedom secured, the decision communicated to the church that had sought it. The verse, brief as it is, marks the resolution of the controversy at its source: the Antioch church, troubled by the Judaizers (v.1), now receives the apostolic decree affirming its freedom from the law. The delivery of the letter to the gathered church completes the Council's work and brings the decision home. The verse displays the fitness of gathering the whole church to hear the decree: the question had troubled all, and so all are assembled to receive the answer. The decree, framed at Jerusalem and confirmed by the delegates, is read to the multitude — the gospel of grace brought home to the church that had sought it, and the controversy answered at its very source.
31:
"Which when they had read, they rejoiced for the consolation." The witnesses note the joy of the Antioch church at the decree. The grammatical voices note the gladness. The church, reading the letter, rejoiced — for the decree freed them from the burden of the law and the trouble of the Judaizers.

On "the consolation," the witnesses note the comfort the decree brought. The Gentile believers, troubled by the demand for circumcision and the law, were comforted by the apostolic decision: they were free, accepted by grace, not bound to the yoke of Moses.

The Reformed Calvin notes the joy as the fruit of the decision: the church rejoiced because the controversy was settled, their liberty affirmed, and their consciences set free from the demand of the law. The decree brought consolation and peace.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Antioch church, reading the letter, rejoiced for the consolation — glad that the decree freed them from the burden of the law and the trouble of the Judaizers. The "consolation" is the comfort the decree brought: the Gentile believers, troubled by the demand for the law, were assured of their freedom and acceptance by grace. The verse displays the joy of the church at the gospel of grace affirmed: the decree, settling the controversy, brought consolation and peace. The verse marks the happy reception of the Council's decision: the Antioch church rejoicing at its freedom from the law. The verse displays the comfort the gospel of grace brings: the believers, freed from the yoke of the law, rejoice in their liberty. The decree, far from a disappointment, was a consolation — the affirmation that the Gentiles are saved by grace, not by the law. The verse displays the fruit of the Council: not division but joy, the church comforted and assured. The Antioch believers, troubled by the Judaizers (v.1), now rejoice in the apostolic decree affirming their freedom — the gospel of grace bringing consolation to the troubled church. The joy at the decree displays the sweetness of Christian liberty: the burden lifted, the conscience freed, the Gentiles received by grace.
32:
"And Judas and Silas, being prophets also themselves, exhorted the brethren with many words, and confirmed them." The witnesses note the ministry of the delegates. On "prophets," the grammatical voices note Judas and Silas had the gift of prophecy (inspired exhortation and teaching). They were not mere bearers of the letter but ministers of the word.

On "exhorted and confirmed," the witnesses note their pastoral ministry. They exhorted the brethren with many words and confirmed (strengthened) them — building up the Antioch church beyond the delivery of the decree. Their prophetic gift was used for the edification of the church.

The Reformed Calvin notes the value of such confirmation: the brethren, having received the decree, were further strengthened by the exhortation of Judas and Silas — the ministry of the word continuing to build up the church.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Judas and Silas, being prophets, exhorted the brethren with many words and confirmed them — not mere bearers of the letter but ministers of the word, using their prophetic gift for the church's edification. The verse displays the ongoing ministry at Antioch: the delegates, beyond delivering the decree, strengthened the church by exhortation. The verse marks the pastoral fruit of the delegation: Judas and Silas, gifted prophets, build up the Antioch church. The verse displays the value of confirmation: the brethren, having received the decree, are further strengthened by the prophets' exhortation. The verse displays the continuing ministry of the word: the decree delivered, the church is then edified by the gifted delegates. Judas and Silas, prophets of Jerusalem, exercise their gift for the Antioch church, confirming the believers in the faith and in their liberty. The verse displays the richness of the early church's ministry: not only apostles and elders but prophets, building up the church by inspired exhortation. The delegates' ministry — exhorting and confirming — completes the work of the Council at Antioch: the decree delivered, the church rejoicing (v.31), and the believers strengthened by the prophets' word. The verse displays the abundance of gifts in the early church: not only the written decree but living prophets to apply it, building up the believers in the faith and their liberty. The ministry of Judas and Silas, exhorting with many words, displays the goal of all true ministry — not merely to inform but to confirm, that the church might stand firm in the gospel of grace.
33:
"And after they had tarried there a space, they were let go in peace from the brethren unto the apostles." The witnesses note the departure of the delegates in peace. The grammatical voices note the cordial dismissal. After a stay at Antioch, Judas and Silas were sent back to Jerusalem "in peace" — a friendly, harmonious parting.

The witnesses note the contrast with the earlier discord. The controversy that began with "no small dissension" (v.2) ends with the delegates dismissed "in peace" — the conflict resolved, harmony restored between Jerusalem and Antioch.

Chrysostom (under v.13) marks the peace: "No more factions and fightings, but thenceforth Paul taught." The Council's decision brought peace; the delegates depart in harmony, the controversy settled.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: after a stay at Antioch, Judas and Silas were dismissed "in peace" to return to Jerusalem — a cordial, harmonious parting. The contrast with the earlier "dissension" (v.2) marks the resolution of the conflict: the controversy that began in strife ends in peace (Chrysostom: "No more factions and fightings"). The verse displays the harmony restored by the Council's decision: the delegates depart in peace, the conflict settled. The verse marks the peaceful close of the Council's work at Antioch: the delegates dismissed in harmony, the controversy resolved. The verse displays the fruit of the wise decision: not lingering division but peace between Jerusalem and Antioch, Jew and Gentile.
