The delegates, having delivered the decree and confirmed the church, depart in peace — the harmony of the church restored. The verse displays the goal achieved: the great controversy over the law settled, the gospel of grace affirmed, and peace restored between the churches. The delegates' departure "in peace" marks the successful resolution of the first great doctrinal crisis: the church united, the Gentiles freed, and harmony restored. The verse displays the blessedness of a controversy rightly settled — peace, not lasting division, the outcome of the Council's Spirit-guided decision. The verse marks the harmonious close of the delegation's work: the men who came from Jerusalem return in peace, the bond between the mother church and the Gentile church strengthened rather than broken. Where the law had threatened division, grace produced concord, and the churches, Jew and Gentile, were knit together in peace.
34:
"Notwithstanding it pleased Silas to abide there still." The brief verse (textually uncertain) notes Silas's decision to remain at Antioch. The grammatical voices note the textual question. Robertson and others note this verse is absent from the best MSS, perhaps added to explain how Silas was available to join Paul (v.40). Whether original or not, Silas did remain (or return) to Antioch.

The witnesses note the providential result. Silas's remaining at Antioch made him available to become Paul's companion on the second journey (v.40), after the breach with Barnabas (v.39). Ellicott (under v.40): "Silas had remained when the other delegates went back. This in itself was a proof of his interest in the mission-work among the Gentiles."

The verse marks the providential positioning of Silas: by remaining at Antioch, he was at hand to replace Barnabas as Paul's companion. The textual uncertainty does not affect the fact, evident from v.40, that Silas was available for the second journey.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Silas remained at Antioch (the verse textually uncertain, absent from the best MSS, perhaps added to explain his availability for v.40, Robertson). The providential result: Silas's remaining made him available to become Paul's companion on the second journey (v.40), after the breach with Barnabas — a proof of his interest in the Gentile mission (Ellicott). The verse displays the providence that positioned Silas at Antioch, at hand to replace Barnabas. The verse marks the quiet preparation for the second journey: Silas, remaining at Antioch, would be chosen by Paul (v.40). The textual uncertainty does not affect the fact, evident from v.40, that Silas was available. The verse displays the providence working behind the scenes: even the breach between Paul and Barnabas (v.39) would be provided for, Silas at hand to accompany Paul. The verse, brief and textually doubtful, nonetheless marks a providential detail: Silas's presence at Antioch, which would make possible the second missionary journey under Paul and Silas. The God who guided the Council also positioned the men for the next stage of the gospel's advance — Silas remaining, ready to be Paul's companion when the time came.
35:
"Paul also and Barnabas continued in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord, with many others also." The witnesses note the continued ministry at Antioch. Chrysostom marks the peace and the teaching: "thenceforth Paul taught." After the controversy was settled, Paul and Barnabas continued at Antioch in the ministry of the word.

The grammatical voices note the rich ministry. Robertson and others note Paul and Barnabas, "with many others," taught and preached at Antioch — a flourishing center of the gospel, with many teachers. The Antioch church, freed from the controversy, prospered in the word.

The witnesses note this was a season of ministry before the second journey. Paul and Barnabas remained at Antioch, teaching and preaching, until Paul proposed to revisit the churches (v.36). The verse marks the fruitful interval between the Council and the second journey.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul and Barnabas continued at Antioch, teaching and preaching the word, with many others — a flourishing ministry after the controversy was settled (Chrysostom: "thenceforth Paul taught"). The Antioch church, freed from the controversy, prospered in the word, with many teachers (Robertson). The verse displays the fruitful season at Antioch: Paul and Barnabas, with many others, ministering the word. The verse marks the interval between the Council and the second journey: a time of teaching and preaching at Antioch. The verse displays the prosperity of the church freed from controversy: the gospel of grace affirmed, the church flourishes in the ministry of the word. Paul and Barnabas, with many others, build up the Antioch church — the great Gentile center of the gospel. The verse displays the peace and fruitfulness that followed the Council's decision: not lingering strife but a flourishing ministry. The verse marks the calm before the second journey: Paul and Barnabas teaching at Antioch, until Paul's proposal to revisit the churches (v.36) would lead to the new mission. The continued ministry at Antioch displays the health of the church after the great controversy: the gospel of grace secured, the believers taught, the church prospering in the word of the Lord.
36:
"And some days after Paul said unto Barnabas, Let us go again and visit our brethren in every city where we have preached the word of the Lord, and see how they do." The witnesses note Paul's proposal for the second journey. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note Paul's pastoral concern: not content with founding churches, he would revisit them to see how they fared and strengthen them.

The grammatical voices note the missionary impulse. Paul proposes to revisit the churches of the first journey — a pastoral visitation to confirm the believers. The "see how they do" displays his fatherly care for the converts.

The witnesses note this proposal would lead, through the dispute over John Mark (vv.37-39), to two missions and the second journey under Paul and Silas (v.40). The pastoral proposal becomes the occasion of both a painful breach and a providential expansion.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul proposed to Barnabas that they revisit the churches of the first journey — a pastoral concern to see how the believers fared and to strengthen them (Henry). The "see how they do" displays Paul's fatherly care for the converts. The verse displays the missionary and pastoral heart of Paul: not content with founding churches, he would confirm them. The verse marks the proposal that would lead, through the dispute over John Mark (vv.37-39), to two missions and the second journey under Paul and Silas. The verse displays Paul's pastoral diligence: the revisiting of the churches to establish them. The proposal, good and pastoral, would become the occasion of the painful breach with Barnabas (v.39) — yet also of a providential expansion (two missions). The verse displays the care of the apostle for the churches he had planted: he would return to see how they fared. The verse marks the beginning of the second journey's narrative: Paul's proposal, the breach with Barnabas, and the new mission. The pastoral concern — "see how they do" — is the model of the faithful minister, who not only wins converts but returns to nurture them. Paul's proposal sets in motion the events that would expand the mission, even through the painful contention that followed.
37:
"And Barnabas determined to take with them John, whose surname was Mark." The witnesses note Barnabas's wish to take John Mark. The grammatical voices note Barnabas's resolve (imperfect tense, persistent). Robertson: "Barnabas willed, wished and stuck to it." Barnabas, Mark's cousin (Colossians 4:10), wished to take him again.

On the kinship, the witnesses note Barnabas's natural affection. Clarke: "John Mark was his sister's son; and natural affection might have led him to the partiality." Ellicott: "The ties of relationship led the cousin to wish to make another trial of his kinsman's fitness."

The Reformed Calvin gives an extended, balanced treatment of the coming dispute, weighing both sides: Barnabas had "a fair color why he should pardon" Mark, who had repented; yet Mark's offense (deserting the work, 13:13) "was greater than it is commonly taken for... he forsook his calling, and was a revolt from the same." Calvin holds both men somewhat exceeded measure, yet sees the providence in it.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Barnabas wished to take John Mark again (persistently, Robertson) — moved by natural affection for his cousin (Clarke, Ellicott, Colossians 4:10), wishing to give him another trial. Calvin gives a balanced treatment: Barnabas had a fair reason to pardon the repentant Mark, yet Mark's offense (deserting the work, 13:13) was graver than commonly thought — a forsaking of his calling. The verse displays the tender side of the coming dispute: Barnabas's affectionate wish to restore his kinsman. The verse marks the beginning of the contention over John Mark: Barnabas, the "son of consolation," would give Mark a second chance, moved by kinship and grace. The verse displays the human element in the apostolic mission: even between holy men, affection and judgment could diverge. Barnabas's wish, generous and forgiving, would clash with Paul's firmer judgment (v.38). The verse displays the providence working through human disagreement: Barnabas's affection for Mark, and Paul's firmness, would lead to two missions. The verse marks the tender root of the dispute — Barnabas's love for his cousin and his desire to restore the one who had failed. The "son of consolation" would consolation Mark; and though the dispute was painful, Mark would be restored and become profitable (2 Timothy 4:11).
38:
"But Paul thought not good to take him with them, who departed from them from Pamphylia, and went not with them to the work." The witnesses note Paul's firm refusal. The grammatical voices note Paul's reasoning. Robertson: "Paul kept on deeming it wise not to be taking along with them this one." Paul's reason: Mark had "departed" (apostatized from, deserted) the work at Pamphylia (13:13).

On Mark's earlier desertion, the witnesses note Paul's judgment. Barnes: "Because he could not confide in his perseverance with them in the toils and perils of their journey." Wesley: "To trust him again, who had deserted them before: who had shrunk from the labour and danger." Paul deemed Mark unfit, having flinched once.

The witnesses note Paul's reason was principled, not personal. Robertson: "Paul had a definite reason for his view... At Perga Mark had faced the same task that Paul and Barnabas did, but he flinched and flickered and quit. Paul declined to repeat the experiment." Paul's firmness rested on Mark's proven unreliability.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul firmly refused to take Mark, because Mark had deserted the work at Pamphylia (13:13) — Paul deeming him unfit, unable to trust one who had flinched and quit (Barnes, Wesley, Robertson). Paul's reason was principled: Mark had faced the task before and failed, and Paul declined to repeat the experiment. The verse displays the firmer side of the dispute: Paul's refusal, grounded in Mark's proven unreliability. The verse marks Paul's firm judgment against taking Mark: the work was too important to risk on one who had deserted. The verse displays the principled basis of Paul's refusal: not personal pique but concern for the mission, which required steadfast companions. Paul, who had himself hazarded his life (v.26), would not entrust the work to a quitter. The verse displays the tension between Barnabas's grace and Paul's firmness: both holy men, yet divided over Mark's fitness. The verse marks the firmer root of the dispute — Paul's insistence on reliability in the work of the gospel. Yet the dispute, painful as it was, would issue in two missions (v.39) and ultimately in Mark's restoration (2 Timothy 4:11). Paul's firmness and Barnabas's grace each had their place, and God overruled the contention for good.
39:
"And the contention was so sharp between them, that they departed asunder one from the other: and so Barnabas took Mark, and sailed unto Cyprus;" The witnesses note the sharp contention and the parting — and divide on whether it was sinful. On "sharp contention" (paroxysm), the grammatical voices note the word. Robertson: "Our very word paroxysm in English... This 'son of consolation' loses his temper in a dispute over his cousin and Paul uses sharp words." Yet the witnesses divide on the moral character.

Some hold there was no sin or anger. Clarke argues the word (paroxysm) need not imply anger: "here, these two apostles differed, and were strenuous, each in support of the part he had adopted... there is no evidence of this [sin] in the text." Wesley: "nothing in the text implies that the sharpness was on both sides... St. Paul, who had the right on his side, maintained it with love."

The witnesses note the providential result. Robertson: "One's judgment may go with Paul, but one's heart goes with Barnabas." The parting issued in two missions: Barnabas with Mark to Cyprus, Paul with Silas (v.40). And Mark was later restored (2 Timothy 4:11; Colossians 4:10).

The witnesses agree: the contention was so sharp (a paroxysm, Robertson) that Paul and Barnabas parted, Barnabas taking Mark to Cyprus. The witnesses divide on the moral character: some (Clarke, Wesley) hold there was no sin or anger — the word need not imply it, and the apostles differed strenuously but without wrong temper (Wesley: Paul maintained the right "with love"); others note the human frailty even in holy men (Robertson: "this 'son of consolation' loses his temper"). The witnesses note the providential result: two missions (Barnabas to Cyprus, Paul with Silas, v.40), and Mark's later restoration (2 Timothy 4:11). The verse displays the painful but providential breach: holy men divided, yet the gospel doubly advanced. The verse marks the separation of Paul and Barnabas: a sharp contention, a painful parting, yet overruled for good. The verse displays the human element in the apostolic mission — even the best men may differ sharply — and the providence that brought good from the breach: two missionary teams instead of one. The witnesses' division (was it sin?) reflects the difficulty of judging: judgment goes with Paul, the heart with Barnabas (Robertson). The verse displays both the frailty and the providence: the contention painful, yet God used it to expand the mission and, in time, to restore Mark, who became profitable to Paul (2 Timothy 4:11). The breach, however regrettable, served the gospel's advance.
40:
"And Paul chose Silas, and departed, being recommended by the brethren unto the grace of God." The witnesses note Paul's new companion and the church's commendation. On Silas, the grammatical voices note he was a fit replacement. Ellicott: "no one, perhaps, could be found so well fitted to fill the place of Barnabas. He too had the gift of prophetic utterance." Robertson notes Silas was a Roman citizen (16:37) and prominent in the Jerusalem church (v.22).

On "recommended by the brethren," the witnesses note the church's support. Clarke and Robertson note the church's sympathy was with Paul: he was commended to the grace of God, as on the first journey (14:26). Robertson: "the sympathy of the church at Antioch is with Paul rather than with Barnabas."

On the providence, Wesley draws the lesson: "how mighty is the grace of God! which in the midst of the world... overcomes all opposition." Paul, commended to grace, departs on the second journey with Silas — the mission continuing despite the breach.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul chose Silas and departed, commended by the brethren to the grace of God. Silas was a fit replacement for Barnabas — gifted in prophecy, a Roman citizen (16:37), prominent in Jerusalem (Ellicott, Robertson). The church's commendation marks its sympathy with Paul (Robertson). The verse displays the providence that continued the mission: Paul, with a new and fitting companion, departs on the second journey. The verse marks the beginning of the second missionary journey: Paul and Silas, commended to grace, set out. The verse displays both the church's support for Paul and the providence that provided Silas. The breach with Barnabas (v.39), painful as it was, did not halt the mission: Paul, with Silas, continues the work, commended to the grace of God. The verse displays the resilience of the gospel's advance: even through the contention, the mission goes on, now in two teams. Paul, commended by the brethren, departs with Silas — the second journey beginning under God's grace. The verse displays the overruling providence: the breach issued in expanded mission, Paul and Silas setting out as Barnabas and Mark sailed to Cyprus. The grace of God, mighty over all opposition (Wesley), carries the mission forward despite the human frailty that divided the apostles.
41:
"And he went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the churches." The witnesses note the beginning of Paul's second journey and his pastoral work. The grammatical voices note the route: through Syria and Cilicia (where Tarsus, Paul's native city, lay), regions where churches already existed (15:23). Gill: "in both these countries he had been before, and had been the instrument of converting many souls, and of planting churches, which he now visited."

On "confirming the churches," the witnesses note the pastoral purpose. As on the return of the first journey (14:22), Paul's aim was to strengthen the believers — establishing the churches in the faith and in their liberty (the decree, 16:4).

The witnesses note the existence of churches in Cilicia, founded by Paul in his earlier years (Galatians 1:21). The second journey begins with the confirming of these churches, carrying the apostolic decree (16:4) and strengthening the believers.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: Paul went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the churches — regions where churches already existed (15:23, founded by Paul in his earlier years, Galatians 1:21, Gill). The pastoral purpose was to strengthen the believers, as on the first journey's return (14:22), establishing them in the faith and their liberty (carrying the decree, 16:4). The verse displays the beginning of the second journey: Paul's pastoral visitation, confirming the churches. The verse marks the start of the great second missionary journey: Paul and Silas, through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the existing churches before pressing on to new fields. The verse displays Paul's pastoral diligence: not only founding churches but confirming them, carrying the apostolic decree that secured the Gentiles' freedom. The verse closes the chapter of the Jerusalem Council with the resumption of the mission: the gospel of grace affirmed, the churches confirmed, and the second journey begun. The verse displays the continuing advance of the gospel: Paul, with Silas, strengthening the churches of Syria and Cilicia, the decree of liberty in hand. The chapter that began with controversy (v.1) ends with the mission resumed and the churches confirmed — the gospel of grace secured and advancing. Paul's second journey, beginning with this pastoral work, would carry the gospel further into the Gentile world.
